title: Credential Reuse Across Multiple Newly-Created Accounts with Clean Transactions
narrative: Fraudster uses a single compromised card to fund multiple newly-created accounts across different platforms or merchants, keeping individual transactions modest to avoid triggering amount-based alerts. Each transaction appears normal in isolation—amounts fit typical spending, velocity per account is low, and billing information validates correctly. The fraud signature emerges in card-level aggregation: C13 and C14 counters reveal the card is being used across far more unique email domains and account contexts than a legitimate cardholder would exhibit. Device fingerprints show first-time pairings (D1=0) with each new account, while C1 and C8 show the card appearing in many distinct transaction windows despite each individual account looking dormant or low-activity. The pattern exploits merchants' focus on per-account behavior while the card itself is being monetized across a portfolio of synthetic or mule accounts.
indicators: C13 > 24 (p90), card used across many distinct email domains
C14 > 7 (p90), card associated with many unique account contexts
D1 = 0, first-time device-card pairing for this transaction
C1 > 60 (p90), elevated card-transaction counter across rolling windows
C8 > 29 (p90), card appearing in many distinct address contexts
card1_distinct_addr1 > 8, card used with multiple billing addresses
TransactionAmt between 50 and 200, within typical spending range to avoid scrutiny
indicator_explanations: The card has been used with many different email addresses in recent history
The card appears across numerous separate account registrations or merchant contexts
This device has never processed a transaction with this card before now
The card shows far more transaction activity than typical cardholder baseline
The card has been paired with many different shipping or billing addresses recently
Billing address associated with the card changes frequently across transactions
Transaction amount appears normal for the merchant category, not raising amount-based flags
summary: A single compromised card is reused to fund multiple newly-created accounts, with each transaction appearing normal but card-level counters revealing usage across many distinct email domains, addresses, and device contexts that no legitimate cardholder would exhibit.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders maintain stable email-card and device-card relationships. When a card appears across many first-time device pairings and diverse email domains while individual transaction behavior remains unremarkable, it indicates the credential is being systematically reused across a portfolio of accounts for monetization or mule activity.
